2: A sustainable society requires the affordable access to energy sources for key services such as lighting, housing, mobility and communication (IPCC, 2011). The European Union is facing unprecedented energy challenges resulting from its dependency on imported fossil fuels and their influence on climate change (Commission of the European Communities, 2006). The availability of different bioenergy production systems should be a main pillar towards the creation of a new energy scheme (Cherubini and Stromman, 2011). The European policy concerning renewable energy has set a goal for supplying 20% of the European energy demand from renewable energy sources by 2020 (EU, 2009).

Biogas, produced from the anaerobic digestion of organic mat- ter, is an option of increasing interest with an expected production of 25% of the bioenergy in the future (Holm-Nielsen and Oleskowiez-Popiel, 2008). Energy production from biogas has

increased all over Europe in recent years (EurObserv'ER, 2010). European countries where the agricultural biogas is most developed are Germany, Italy, Denmark, Austria and Sweden (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009). Biogas production from animal manure has been proved to reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, espe- cially ammonia and methane derived from manure storage facili- ties (Holm-Nielsen and Oleskowiez-Popiel, 2008). In addition, it avoids the pollution of surface and ground water resources (Holm-Nielsen and Oleskowiez-Popiel, 2008). Beyond the benefits aforementioned, the anaerobic digestion of biomass produces renewable energy and organic fertiliser, which implies reduction in the demand of mineral fertilisers (Berglund, 2006; Carrosio, 2013).

Public policies have played an important role in stimulating and shaping the spread of biogas plants. For example, in Italy important incentives are given for electricity production from biogas such as green certificates and feed-in tariffs (Carrosio, 2013). As a result, there are more than one thousand farm-scale biogas plants in this country (Fabbri et al., 2013), mainly located in the Po Valley region (Northern Italy). This area is characterised by a high concentration

of large livestock farms and intensive cultivation of cereals (Carrosio, 2013). Around 18% of Italian biogas plants process live- stock manure, 20% cereal crops and 62% use both types of feedstock (Carrosio, 2013). There is a reason behind this distribution: the anaerobic digestion of manure renders lower biogas yield than other substrates such as energy crops (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2007). To counteract this drawback, higher biogas yield can be achieved through its co-digestion with other substrates such as cereal crops (De al., 2012b). Thus, feedstock choice is an important fac- tor to determine the power capacity of the plant (Carrosio, 2013).

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is an internationally accepted method to gain insight into the environmental aspects of a product or system (ISO 14040, 2006). A number of LCA studies can be found in the literature analysing the environmental performance of bio- gas based systems. In these studies, large variations in energy per- formance and environmental impacts were found among the different biogas systems, which can be attributed to the variability of feedstock, different cultivation systems of energy crops (Bauer et al., 2010; Dressler et al., 2012), final use of energy or manage- ment schemes for the digestate (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; De Vries et al., 2012b; Dressler et al., 2012; Poeschl et al., 2012). Hence, it is not possible to draw any general conclusion on the environmental impact of bioenergy production without clearly defining the system boundaries considered (Berglund, 2006).

The objective of this study was to assess the environmental per- formance of a co-digestion plant operating with pig slurry and maize silage. The analysis included the assessment and quan- tification of inputs, energy flows and emissions during the entire life cycle of the biogas production and use. Moreover, the main fac- tors with the largest impacts on the environmental performance and energy produced were identified and analysed in detail. The main outcomes of the anaerobic co-digestion process were com- pared with anaerobic mono-digestion in order to understand the opportunities offered by the simultaneous digestion of two or more different feedstocks.

7: An LCA study largely depends on the assumptions made regard- ing data quality, system boundaries and allocation method. Awareness of the implications of these methodological aspects is therefore important in properly interpreting the results (Berglund, 2006).
8: LCA has been the methodology selected for the environmental analysis performed in this study. LCA is a standardised method which guarantees the reproducibility of the results obtained (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006).

For this purpose, detailed data regarding the consumption of natural resources and the emissions to air, water and soil linked to the life cycle of the product was collected, from raw material acquisition through the phases of production and use to waste management (ISO 14040, 2006).
9: As aforementioned, the goal of this study was to evaluate the environmental profile of a biogas based bioenergy system from a cradle-to-gate perspective. To do so, a real Italian biogas plant which co-digests pig slurry and maize silage was assessed in detail. Specific objectives included the identification of the most critical stages (environmental hotspots) in order to identify opportunities to attain environmental benefits as well as to compare the
environmental performance of this biogas plant with two plants in which pig slurry and maize silage are digested separately (Lijó et al., 2014).

The Po Valley is the most important Italian agricultural area and it also comprises the most industrialised regions of Northern Italy (Piedmont, Lombardy, Emilia Romagna and Veneto). The plant under study is located in the district of Vercelli (Piedmont region). It has an electrical power of 999 kWe and codigests maize silage (55% in mass) and pig slurry (45% in mass). The life cycle environ- mental impacts were determined by building a Life Cycle Inventory (LCI), that is, the identification and quantification of all relevant inputs and outputs flows of the bioenergy system.
10: According to the ISO standards, the functional unit (FU) is defined as the main function of the system expressed in quantita- tive terms and provides the reference to which all the inputs and outputs of the product system are calculated (ISO 14040, 2006). The main function of this bioenergy system is the production of biogas and its further conversion into electricity and heat. Therefore, the FU chosen was the average biogas production per day: 11,436 of biogas.
11: The system boundaries define which processes are included in the analysis. The system was subdivided in different subsystems (abbreviated as SS) for better understanding. The system bound- aries include maize biomass production (SS1), feedstock supply (SS2), bioenergy production (SS3) and digestate management (SS4) as well as avoided processes such as conventional manage- ment of pig slurry, electricity production and mineral fertilisation. The study comprises the production of inputs used in these pro- cesses, such as chemicals, diesel fuel, electricity and infrastructure. All processes considered within the bioenergy system under study are outlined in Fig. 1.

As shown in Fig. 1, the production of pig slurry was excluded from the system boundaries because it was considered as a waste from pig breeding farms. Therefore, its production is independent of its utilisation as a fuel for anaerobic digestion (De Vries et al., 2012a).
12: This subsystem includes all agricultural activities involved within the cultivation of maize, from field preparation to the har- vesting operations. Maize is extensively cultivated in the Po Valley region for both energy and food/feed purposes (Fabbri et al., 2013; González-García et al., 2013). Nevertheless, mainly due to the Italian subsidy framework for electricity generation from renewable sources, most biogas plants built in Italy before 2013 are fed with cereal silage and, in particular, with maize silage since it is the most productive energy crop. Specifically, strong public incentives were granted for electricity produced from biogas plants operating before 31 December 2012 and with an electrical power lower than 1 MW (280 MW of electricity fed into the grid). From 1 January 2013, the incentives (236 MW of elec- tricity fed into the grid) are granted to plants with an electrical power lower than kW, fed mainly with by-products (minimum 70% of the biomass fed to the digesters) (Negri et al., 2014). As a result, around 10% of total maize cultivation in this area is dedi- cated to biogas production (Casati, 2013). Thus, in this study it was assumed that the maize requirement for food and feed is com- pletely satisfied. Nitrogen and phosphorus emissions derived from the application of fertilisers (digestate and urea) were also
Emissions from the combustion of diesel and biogas

Fig. 1. Flowchart and system boundaries of the bioenergy system under study. Note that processes outside the dotted box are excluded from the system boundaries and grey boxes indicate avoided processes.
included in the study as well as the uptake of carbon dioxide dur- ing maize cultivation.

This subsystem includes the delivery of the feedstock to the bio- gas plant. In the case of pig slurry, it is daily delivered by a tractor coupled with a slurry tank (2 km). Thus, its storage in the biogas plant was assumed negligible. Regarding maize biomass, it is trans- ported by lorry (5 km) and it is ensiled and stored inside the biogas plant. It was assumed that 2% of the maize produced is lost during storage (Bacenetti et al., 2014).

It was assumed that 2% of the maize produced is lost during storage (Bacenetti et al., 2014).
14: This subsystem includes all agricultural activities involved within the cultivation of maize, from field preparation to the har- vesting operations. Maize is extensively cultivated in the Po Valley region for both energy and food/feed purposes (Fabbri et al., 2013; González-García et al., 2013). Nevertheless, mainly due to the Italian subsidy framework for electricity generation from renewable sources, most biogas plants built in Italy before 2013 are fed with cereal silage and, in particular, with maize silage since it is the most productive energy crop. Specifically, strong public incentives were granted for electricity produced from biogas plants operating before 31 December 2012 and with an electrical power lower than 1 MW (280 MW of electricity fed into the grid). From 1 January 2013, the incentives (236 MW of elec- tricity fed into the grid) are granted to plants with an electrical power lower than kW, fed mainly with by-products (minimum 70% of the biomass fed to the digesters) (Negri et al., 2014). As a result, around 10% of total maize cultivation in this area is dedi- cated to biogas production (Casati, 2013). Thus, in this study it was assumed that the maize requirement for food and feed is com- pletely satisfied. Nitrogen and phosphorus emissions derived from the application of fertilisers (digestate and urea) were also
Emissions from the combustion of diesel and biogas

Fig. 1. Flowchart and system boundaries of the bioenergy system under study. Note that processes outside the dotted box are excluded from the system boundaries and grey boxes indicate avoided processes.
included in the study as well as the uptake of carbon dioxide dur- ing maize cultivation.

This subsystem includes the delivery of the feedstock to the bio- gas plant. In the case of pig slurry, it is daily delivered by a tractor coupled with a slurry tank (2 km). Thus, its storage in the biogas plant was assumed negligible. Regarding maize biomass, it is trans- ported by lorry (5 km) and it is ensiled and stored inside the biogas plant. It was assumed that 2% of the maize produced is lost during storage (Bacenetti et al., 2014).
15: This subsystem encompassed the anaerobic digestion of the feedstock including biogas and digestate production, the conver- sion of biogas into electricity and heat in a combined heat and power generation (CHP) engine and the separation of the digestate into liquid and solid fractions by means of a screw press separator. The maize silage is loaded by a tractor to a screw auger, from which it is fed into the digester. Pig slurry is introduced by a lobe pump. A fraction of the liquid stream of the produced digestate is used to dilute the mixture in the digester.

Biogas is produced under mesophilic conditions (40 °C) in a continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR). The hydraulic retention time and the organic loading rate, expressed in terms of volatile solids (VS) are 2.07 kg VS day and 35-50 days, respectively. The temperature is kept homogenous by the circulation of hot water coming from the engine jacket. Each digester has 4 mixers and a pump for the discharge of the digestate. The physic-chemical characteristics of the feedstock used as well as the digestate pro- duced (including raw digestate prior to separation and its liquid and solid fraction) are summarised in Table 1.

The biogas is then purified in a sand filter, dehumidified and desulphurised. The dehumidification process is carried out in a refrigeration unit and a wet scrubber with sodium hydroxide assures the removal of sulphur compounds from the biogas stream.
Physico-chemical characterisation of the feedstock used for biogas production as well as of the raw digestate produced and its liquid and solid fraction.

Net bioenergy is produced by the biogas that is burnt in an internal combustion engine CHP with an electrical power of 999 kWe, an electrical efficiency of 41% and a thermal efficiency of 44%. The electricity produced by CHP is fed into the national grid, whereas part of the produced heat is used in the facilities to keep digester temperature and surplus heat is wasted to the atmosphere. Other inputs such as electricity, sodium hydroxide and lubricant oil are required and outputs such as biogas losses (1.5% of biogas produced) and combustion emissions are released to the atmosphere. It is interesting to remark that the electricity input required for the biogas plant is taken from the national grid.

25: The performance of this bioenergy system entails avoided pro- cesses due to the valorisation of some streams. It was assumed an avoided electricity production from the Italian electric profile equivalent to the electricity produced from the biogas. The solid fraction of the digestate and part of the liquid fraction are used as organic fertilisers in arable land. Thus, both the equivalent pro- duction of ammonium nitrate, triple superphosphate and potas- sium sulphate as well as their application on land were considered as avoided processes. In addition, it was also considered that the valorisation of pig slurry by anaerobic digestion avoids its conventional management. Traditionally, pig manure has been applied on land in Northern Italy due to its fertiliser potential. However, regional legislation sets a minimum period of storage in order to assure health security and environmental protection (Ministero delle Politiche Agricole e Forestali, 2006). Thus, it was considered that the conventional management of pig slurry con- sists on its storage close to the farm, its transport to the field as well as its spreading on land as an organic fertiliser.
26: Site-specific data should be used, when available, to better reflect the real situation when bioenergy systems are evaluated. For this reason, primary specific data concerning inputs (diesel fuel, feedstock, electricity, heat, sodium hydroxide and lubricant oil) and outputs (biogas, digestate, heat and electricity) for SS2, SS3 and SS4 were supplied by plant workers and they reflect aver- age data for the year 2011.

Regarding SS1, inputs (digestate, fertilisers, pesticides, water and diesel) and outputs (field emissions derived from fertilisers application) related to all agricultural activities for maize cultiva- tion were taken from González-García et al. (2013), where real data coming from the maize plantations under study were evaluated. As in the mentioned study, carbon dioxide uptaken by photosynthesis was taken into account. It was estimated according to dry matter
content (38%) and carbon content in the dry biomass (41.5%) (González-García et al., 2013).

Different transport distances were considered within this bioenergy system. Maize silage is delivered 5 km up to the biogas plant, similarly for the delivery of the solid and liquid fraction of the digestate from the farm to the field. Regarding pig slurry and its conventional management, the transport distance considered is only 2 km.

Within the application of any type of fertilisers (mineral fer- tilisers, digestate and pig slurry), derived field emissions were also estimated. Nitrogen-based emissions (ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitrogen and nitrate) were calculated with factors provided by Brentrup et al. (2000). Phosphate emissions to water were esti- mated according to Rossier and Charles (1998). Emissions asso- ciated with the avoided conventional management of pig slurry (methane, ammonia and nitrous oxide) were calculated with the factors provided by ISPRA (2008), assuming an average production of 10 kg of pig slurry per head and day (Negri et al., 2014). Combustion emissions derived from the CHP were taken from NERI (2010). Biogas losses and nitrogen-based compounds derived from digestate storage were estimated according to De Vries et al. (2012b). The quantity of mineral fertilisers that are potentially replaced by the digestate was calculated on the basis of digestate composition and fertiliser replacement values reported by De Vries et al. (2012a).

Finally, background data regarding the production of all required inputs such as diesel fuel, sodium hydroxide, lubricant oil and electricity, as well as replaced fertilisers, were taken from ecoinvent® database (Althaus et al., 2007; Dones et al., 2007; Jungbluth et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Spiermann et al., 2007). A detailed description of LCI data of each subsystem can be found in Tables 2-6.

30: Within the Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA), the following impact categories were considered for evaluation: climate change (CC), ozone depletion (OD), terrestrial acidification (TA), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), photochemical oxidant formation (POF), agricultural land occupation (ALO) and
Table 3 Global inventory data of Subsystem 2 (data per functional unit).

Inputs from technosphere|Inputs from technosphere|Inputs from technosphere|Inputs from technosphere|Outputs to technosphere|Outputs to technosphere |Maize|Pig slurry||Outputs to technosphere|Outputs to technosphere Materials and fuels Maize biomass (from SS1)|55.1 t||Primary data|"silage Products Maize slurry Pig"|"55.1 Primary data 45 m³" Pig slurry||45 m³|Primary data|"silage Products Maize slurry Pig"|"55.1 Primary data 45 m³" Diesel fuel|24.2 kg|27.0 kg||| Tractor|1.80 kg|1.33 kg||| Agricultural tillage|1.86 kg|494 kg||| Transport Lorry|24.2 t km||Primary data||
fossil depletion (FD). The characterisation factors considered were those from ReCiPe Midpoint methodology (Goedkoop et al., 2009).

Carbon dioxide from biogenic sources could be considered as climate-neutral since, according to the natural carbon cycle, the equivalent amount of carbon dioxide emitted from an organic source was previously uptaken by photosynthesis. In agreement with the literature (González-García et al., 2013; Wiloso et al., 2012), the carbon dioxide absorbed during maize growth has been considered (characterisation factor -1). Correspondingly, biogenic carbon dioxide derived from maize and pig slurry transformation into electricity (such as carbon dioxide emissions from biogas burning in the CHP, uncontrolled biogas losses or residual biogas production from digestate storage) has been taken into account (characterisation factor 1). A wide variety of feedstock could be used for biogas production, each one implying different advantages and disadvantages. In this way, climate change mitigation poten- tial of maize utilisation as a feedstock for biogas production is anal- ysed. In addition, it allows the comparison with other energy crops with a different capacity of carbon sequestration.

fossil depletion (FD). The characterisation factors considered were those from ReCiPe Midpoint methodology (Goedkoop et al., 2009).

Carbon dioxide from biogenic sources could be considered as climate-neutral since, according to the natural carbon cycle, the equivalent amount of carbon dioxide emitted from an organic source was previously uptaken by photosynthesis. In agreement with the literature (González-García et al., 2013; Wiloso et al., 2012), the carbon dioxide absorbed during maize growth has been considered (characterisation factor -1). Correspondingly, biogenic carbon dioxide derived from maize and pig slurry transformation into electricity (such as carbon dioxide emissions from biogas burning in the CHP, uncontrolled biogas losses or residual biogas production from digestate storage) has been taken into account (characterisation factor 1). A wide variety of feedstock could be used for biogas production, each one implying different advantages and disadvantages. In this way, climate change mitigation poten- tial of maize utilisation as a feedstock for biogas production is anal- ysed. In addition, it allows the comparison with other energy crops with a different capacity of carbon sequestration.
32: Table 7 shows the characterisation results for the bioenergy system under study corresponding to the functional unit

(11,436 m³ of biogas produced that is the average biogas produc- tion per day). Furthermore, Fig. 2 presents the relative con- tributions of each subsystem and avoided process to the environmental profile of the system.

The environmental impacts of maize biomass production (SS1) have an important influence on the environmental profile of the system, with contributions ranging from 5.1% (ME) to 92% (ALO). Agricultural activities were mainly responsible for the impacts on OD, FE, POF and FD, whereas nitrogen-based emissions derived from fertilisation were the main responsible for the impacts on TA and ME (especially ammonia). Otherwise, ALO is influenced by the occupation of the arable land associated to maize cultiva- tion. The environmental burdens from SS1 present a different behaviour in CC since the carbon dioxide uptaken during maize growth brings environmental credits, offsetting the GHG emissions along the system. Moreover, in this study 2% of silage loss has been considered. This value is achieved when ensiling is properly carried out (current situation). However, silage losses can be higher. If 10% of silage losses were considered, more maize straw would be needed and more maize would be ensiled, thus increasing the environmental impacts derived from SS1 (8% higher) and SS2 (3% higher).
The environmental impacts of feedstock supply (SS2) have an unremarkable contribution on the system profile, at most this sub- system produces 2.5% of the impacts on POF.

Environmental impacts from bioenergy production (SS3) spe- cially contributed to CC (34%) and POF (37%), due to combustion emissions released during the biogas burnt in the CHP (specially, carbon dioxide in CC and nitrogen oxides in POF).

Digestate management (SS4) has an important influence in TA (28%) and ME (47%) owing to the application on land of both solid and liquid fractions from the digestate. Regarding TA, this impact is mainly linked to ammonia emissions, whereas the impact pro- duced in ME is due to nitrate emissions to water.

Finally, environmental credits associated with avoided pro- cesses also contribute to offset the environmental burdens. Firstly, these positive effects are associated with the avoided con- ventional management of pig slurry (19% in TA and ME) as well as the avoided electricity production (19% in CC, 43% in OD, 29% in FE, 20% in POF and 44% in FD). Specifically, emissions of ammo- nia during storage and land application as well as nitrate leaching derived from land application are the main environmental impacts

related with the conventional management of pig slurry while the environmental burdens of electricity production are related with the high ratio of non-renewable sources in the Italian electric pro- file. In addition, the avoided mineral fertilisation due to the use of the digestate brings remarkable credits in ME (33%), due to avoided nitrate emissions to water.

The base scenario (BS) previously analysed was compared with different alternative scenarios in order to determine how potential improvement actions could affect the environmental profile of the biogas plant under study.

As mentioned before, heat is produced together with electricity in the CHP. As in many Italian biogas plans, electricity is totally sold to the Italian grid, while heat is only partially recycled in heat- ing the anaerobic reactors. The full exploitation of surplus heat is complicated since the amount of heat produced varies according to weather conditions and it can be only carry out when the recep- tor is close enough to the CHP. However, the surplus heat repre- sents a significant share of the total amount produced by the

CHP. This heat might be used to heat pigsties or nearby green- houses during the coldest months, to dry forage, to drive a small Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) turbine or an absorption chiller. In this alternative scenario (AS1), surplus heat was considered to be used in two greenhouses from October to March, resulting in environmental credits due to avoided heat produced in a diesel boiler. Each greenhouse has a global area of 1 ha and it is used to grown tomato. The infrastructure required for transmitting the heat to the greenhouses is considered negligible, since, according to our results, the contribution of the infrastructure corresponding to the biogas plant is up to 3% of the total impact produced.
3.2.2. Covering the liquid digestate storage tank (AS2)

Each fraction of the digestate (solid and liquid) is separately stored. Biogas as well as nitrogen-based emissions were identified since storage was considered in open tank for the liquid fraction and in pile for the solid fraction. In this alternative scenario (AS2), covering the storage tank of the digestate liquid fraction was proposed in order to decreased derived emissions (80%) as well as to take advantage of the residual biogas (ISPRA, 2008; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014). This biogas is recirculated to the pro- cess resulting in light increase (0.14%) of electricity production and avoided electricity from the grid. In addition, it was assumed that an airtight cover on the storage tank would decrease emissions of N-based compounds (Wulf et al., 2006). Therefore, only 20% of emissions computed for the storage of liquid fraction are realised in this alternative scenario.

Emissions during and after digestate application on arable land are highly influenced by the application practise used (Wulf et al., 2006). As base case, surface spreading was considered as applica- tion technique of digestate because it is the most widespread pro- cedure for organic fertilisers in the Po Valley (78%) (ISPRA, 2008). However, injection is also carried out but to a lesser extent. According to Wulf et al. (2006), when injection of the digestate is performed, ammonia emissions are nearly half of these from sur- face spreading with a splash plate, because of the decrease in ammonia volatilisation (Wulf et al., 2002a). However, nitrous oxide emissions are around 2.7 times higher due to the promotion of anaerobic sites (Wulf et al., 2002b). In addition, the consumption of diesel fuel is higher when injection is applied compared with surface spreading because the slurry tank is equipped with a cou- ple of anchors working at 20-25 cm depth. The comparison among BS and each alternative scenario under study (AS1, AS2 and AS3) is shown in Fig. 3.

As it can be seen, environmental credits derived from surplus heat valorisation (AS1) significantly influenced the definition of system profile, especially in energy related categories such as CC, OD and FD. The reduction of 80% in emissions when the storage tank of the liquid fraction of digestate is covered (AS2) had a posi- tive effect in impact categories such as CC due to avoided emis- sions of residual biogas as well as TA and ME due to the decrease of ammonia emissions. Finally, the increase of nitrous oxide emis- sions related with liquid fraction of digestate injection in arable land (AS3) reduced the positive effect achieved in CC due to the increased emissions of nitrous oxide. However, the results for TA
Fig. 3. Comparative environmental profiles per impact category between alter- native scenarios under study.

Fig. 4. Comparative environmental profiles per impact category between three bioenergy plants. Plant A - Codigestion (pig slurry + maize silage); Plant B - Monodigestion (pig slurry); Plant C - Monodigestion (maize silage).
were better compared with BS due to the reduction of ammonia emissions.

The results obtained regarding the environmental profile of the bioenergy plant under study (Plant A), which codigests maize silage and pig slurry, can be compared with those obtained in a previous study (Lijó et al., 2014), where two mono-digestion plants were assessed, one based on pig slurry as substrate (Plant B) and the other on maize silage (Plant C). It is important to remark that the cultivation of the maize used in plant A and in plant C is not exactly the same, since different amount of herbicides, water for irrigation, fertilisers (and thus, different derived emissions) are applied, resulting in different maize yield per hectare. Moreover, it is important to note that both plants A and C include a separation step of the raw digestate, resulting in liquid and solid fraction. In both plants, the solid fraction is stored, transported and applied on land as an organic fertiliser by means of manure spreader. The difference relies on the management of the liquid fraction because in plant A, differently to plant B, not all the digestate liquid fraction is recirculated to the digester, being part of this fraction also stored, delivered and used as a fertiliser. Liquid digestate is recirculated into the digester in order to keep dry matter concen- tration under 10% (Probiogás, 2011). Fig. 4 displays the compara- tive environmental effects between the three mentioned bioenergy plants. According to the results reported in Fig. 4, the use of maize for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant C) rendered the worst results in all impact categories. Nonetheless, the utilisation of pig slurry as a substrate for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant B) achieved the best environmental profile. The anaerobic co-digestion of both sub- strates (Plant A) was ranked in an intermediate position. The reason behind the performance of Plant B is mainly related with the absence of a cultivation step (SS1), which implied high

environmental burdens as well as environmental credits accrued by the avoided pig slurry conventional management and mineral fertilisation. As shown in Table 1, the specific biogas production of pig slurry is lower compared with maize. In addition, for the same amount of biogas, much more feedstock needs to be digested in plant B (Table 8).

In addition, in Table 8 it can also be seen that in Plant C less amount of feedstock is required in order to produce the same vol- ume of than Plant A. However, the higher electrical efficiency of Plant A implied lower specific biogas consumption. Moreover, environmental credits such as those from the avoided conventional management of pig slurry contributed to a better environmental profile in Plant A.

Additionally, the cumulative energy demand (CED) was calcu- lated in order to determine the quantity of non-renewable energy required in each biogas plant to produce the output electricity (Hischier et al., 2009). The results of CED are presented in Table 8 in two different ways. Firstly, global results including all processes defined within the system boundaries (see Fig. 1) are shown and then CED was calculated excluding the avoided pro- cesses in order to determine the real energy consumption of each plant. As expected, for the three plants under assessment the val- ues for CED were lower when avoided processes were included because of the environmental credits associated. Using these val- ues of CED (without the avoided processes), the energy efficiency of the bioenergy plants was calculated in terms of Energy Return on Investment (EROI), which is defined as the ratio of the energy delivered by a process to the energy used directly and indirectly in the same process. The results indicate that, although Plant B is the plant which less bioenergy produced per functional unit, the relationship between the energy input and output is higher in

comparison with the other two plants. The rationale behind these results is related to the non-renewable energy consumption during the cultivation step (SS1) in Plant A and C. Specifically, this subsys- tem provided around 90% of the burdens of CED when avoided pro- cesses were excluded from the calculations due to energy consumed in the agricultural activities (mainly due to the high energy input required for irrigation operations, mainly in electric- ity, diesel and machinery).

The anaerobic co-digestion of both sub- strates (Plant A) was ranked in an intermediate position. The reason behind the performance of Plant B is mainly related with the absence of a cultivation step (SS1), which implied high

environmental burdens as well as environmental credits accrued by the avoided pig slurry conventional management and mineral fertilisation. As shown in Table 1, the specific biogas production of pig slurry is lower compared with maize. In addition, for the same amount of biogas, much more feedstock needs to be digested in plant B (Table 8).

In addition, in Table 8 it can also be seen that in Plant C less amount of feedstock is required in order to produce the same vol- ume of than Plant A. However, the higher electrical efficiency of Plant A implied lower specific biogas consumption. Moreover, environmental credits such as those from the avoided conventional management of pig slurry contributed to a better environmental profile in Plant A.

Additionally, the cumulative energy demand (CED) was calcu- lated in order to determine the quantity of non-renewable energy required in each biogas plant to produce the output electricity (Hischier et al., 2009). The results of CED are presented in Table 8 in two different ways. Firstly, global results including all processes defined within the system boundaries (see Fig. 1) are shown and then CED was calculated excluding the avoided pro- cesses in order to determine the real energy consumption of each plant. As expected, for the three plants under assessment the val- ues for CED were lower when avoided processes were included because of the environmental credits associated. Using these val- ues of CED (without the avoided processes), the energy efficiency of the bioenergy plants was calculated in terms of Energy Return on Investment (EROI), which is defined as the ratio of the energy delivered by a process to the energy used directly and indirectly in the same process. The results indicate that, although Plant B is the plant which less bioenergy produced per functional unit, the relationship between the energy input and output is higher in

comparison with the other two plants. The rationale behind these results is related to the non-renewable energy consumption during the cultivation step (SS1) in Plant A and C. Specifically, this subsys- tem provided around 90% of the burdens of CED when avoided pro- cesses were excluded from the calculations due to energy consumed in the agricultural activities (mainly due to the high energy input required for irrigation operations, mainly in electric- ity, diesel and machinery).

related with the conventional management of pig slurry while the environmental burdens of electricity production are related with the high ratio of non-renewable sources in the Italian electric pro- file. In addition, the avoided mineral fertilisation due to the use of the digestate brings remarkable credits in ME (33%), due to avoided nitrate emissions to water.

The base scenario (BS) previously analysed was compared with different alternative scenarios in order to determine how potential improvement actions could affect the environmental profile of the biogas plant under study.

As mentioned before, heat is produced together with electricity in the CHP. As in many Italian biogas plans, electricity is totally sold to the Italian grid, while heat is only partially recycled in heat- ing the anaerobic reactors. The full exploitation of surplus heat is complicated since the amount of heat produced varies according to weather conditions and it can be only carry out when the recep- tor is close enough to the CHP. However, the surplus heat repre- sents a significant share of the total amount produced by the

CHP. This heat might be used to heat pigsties or nearby green- houses during the coldest months, to dry forage, to drive a small Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) turbine or an absorption chiller. In this alternative scenario (AS1), surplus heat was considered to be used in two greenhouses from October to March, resulting in environmental credits due to avoided heat produced in a diesel boiler. Each greenhouse has a global area of 1 ha and it is used to grown tomato. The infrastructure required for transmitting the heat to the greenhouses is considered negligible, since, according to our results, the contribution of the infrastructure corresponding to the biogas plant is up to 3% of the total impact produced.
3.2.2. Covering the liquid digestate storage tank (AS2)

Each fraction of the digestate (solid and liquid) is separately stored. Biogas as well as nitrogen-based emissions were identified since storage was considered in open tank for the liquid fraction and in pile for the solid fraction. In this alternative scenario (AS2), covering the storage tank of the digestate liquid fraction was proposed in order to decreased derived emissions (80%) as well as to take advantage of the residual biogas (ISPRA, 2008; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014). This biogas is recirculated to the pro- cess resulting in light increase (0.14%) of electricity production and avoided electricity from the grid. In addition, it was assumed that an airtight cover on the storage tank would decrease emissions of N-based compounds (Wulf et al., 2006). Therefore, only 20% of emissions computed for the storage of liquid fraction are realised in this alternative scenario.

Emissions during and after digestate application on arable land are highly influenced by the application practise used (Wulf et al., 2006). As base case, surface spreading was considered as applica- tion technique of digestate because it is the most widespread pro- cedure for organic fertilisers in the Po Valley (78%) (ISPRA, 2008). However, injection is also carried out but to a lesser extent. According to Wulf et al. (2006), when injection of the digestate is performed, ammonia emissions are nearly half of these from sur- face spreading with a splash plate, because of the decrease in ammonia volatilisation (Wulf et al., 2002a). However, nitrous oxide emissions are around 2.7 times higher due to the promotion of anaerobic sites (Wulf et al., 2002b). In addition, the consumption of diesel fuel is higher when injection is applied compared with surface spreading because the slurry tank is equipped with a cou- ple of anchors working at 20-25 cm depth. The results obtained regarding the environmental profile of the bioenergy plant under study (Plant A), which codigests maize silage and pig slurry, can be compared with those obtained in a previous study (Lijó et al., 2014), where two mono-digestion plants were assessed, one based on pig slurry as substrate (Plant B) and the other on maize silage (Plant C). It is important to remark that the cultivation of the maize used in plant A and in plant C is not exactly the same, since different amount of herbicides, water for irrigation, fertilisers (and thus, different derived emissions) are applied, resulting in different maize yield per hectare. Moreover, it is important to note that both plants A and C include a separation step of the raw digestate, resulting in liquid and solid fraction. In both plants, the solid fraction is stored, transported and applied on land as an organic fertiliser by means of manure spreader. The difference relies on the management of the liquid fraction because in plant A, differently to plant B, not all the digestate liquid fraction is recirculated to the digester, being part of this fraction also stored, delivered and used as a fertiliser. Liquid digestate is recirculated into the digester in order to keep dry matter concen- tration under 10% (Probiogás, 2011). Fig. 4 displays the compara- tive environmental effects between the three mentioned bioenergy plants. According to the results reported in Fig. 4, the use of maize for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant C) rendered the worst results in all impact categories. Nonetheless, the utilisation of pig slurry as a substrate for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant B) achieved the best environmental profile. The anaerobic co-digestion of both sub- strates (Plant A) was ranked in an intermediate position. related with the conventional management of pig slurry while the environmental burdens of electricity production are related with the high ratio of non-renewable sources in the Italian electric pro- file. In addition, the avoided mineral fertilisation due to the use of the digestate brings remarkable credits in ME (33%), due to avoided nitrate emissions to water.

The base scenario (BS) previously analysed was compared with different alternative scenarios in order to determine how potential improvement actions could affect the environmental profile of the biogas plant under study.

As mentioned before, heat is produced together with electricity in the CHP. As in many Italian biogas plans, electricity is totally sold to the Italian grid, while heat is only partially recycled in heat- ing the anaerobic reactors. The full exploitation of surplus heat is complicated since the amount of heat produced varies according to weather conditions and it can be only carry out when the recep- tor is close enough to the CHP. However, the surplus heat repre- sents a significant share of the total amount produced by the

CHP. This heat might be used to heat pigsties or nearby green- houses during the coldest months, to dry forage, to drive a small Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) turbine or an absorption chiller. In this alternative scenario (AS1), surplus heat was considered to be used in two greenhouses from October to March, resulting in environmental credits due to avoided heat produced in a diesel boiler. Each greenhouse has a global area of 1 ha and it is used to grown tomato. The infrastructure required for transmitting the heat to the greenhouses is considered negligible, since, according to our results, the contribution of the infrastructure corresponding to the biogas plant is up to 3% of the total impact produced.
3.2.2. Covering the liquid digestate storage tank (AS2)

Each fraction of the digestate (solid and liquid) is separately stored. Biogas as well as nitrogen-based emissions were identified since storage was considered in open tank for the liquid fraction and in pile for the solid fraction. In this alternative scenario (AS2), covering the storage tank of the digestate liquid fraction was proposed in order to decreased derived emissions (80%) as well as to take advantage of the residual biogas (ISPRA, 2008; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014). This biogas is recirculated to the pro- cess resulting in light increase (0.14%) of electricity production and avoided electricity from the grid. In addition, it was assumed that an airtight cover on the storage tank would decrease emissions of N-based compounds (Wulf et al., 2006). Therefore, only 20% of emissions computed for the storage of liquid fraction are realised in this alternative scenario.

Emissions during and after digestate application on arable land are highly influenced by the application practise used (Wulf et al., 2006). As base case, surface spreading was considered as applica- tion technique of digestate because it is the most widespread pro- cedure for organic fertilisers in the Po Valley (78%) (ISPRA, 2008). However, injection is also carried out but to a lesser extent. According to Wulf et al. (2006), when injection of the digestate is performed, ammonia emissions are nearly half of these from sur- face spreading with a splash plate, because of the decrease in ammonia volatilisation (Wulf et al., 2002a). However, nitrous oxide emissions are around 2.7 times higher due to the promotion of anaerobic sites (Wulf et al., 2002b). In addition, the consumption of diesel fuel is higher when injection is applied compared with surface spreading because the slurry tank is equipped with a cou- ple of anchors working at 20-25 cm depth. The comparison among BS and each alternative scenario under study (AS1, AS2 and AS3) is shown in Fig. 3.

As it can be seen, environmental credits derived from surplus heat valorisation (AS1) significantly influenced the definition of system profile, especially in energy related categories such as CC, OD and FD. The reduction of 80% in emissions when the storage tank of the liquid fraction of digestate is covered (AS2) had a posi- tive effect in impact categories such as CC due to avoided emis- sions of residual biogas as well as TA and ME due to the decrease of ammonia emissions. Finally, the increase of nitrous oxide emis- sions related with liquid fraction of digestate injection in arable land (AS3) reduced the positive effect achieved in CC due to the increased emissions of nitrous oxide. However, the results for TA
Fig. 3. Comparative environmental profiles per impact category between alter- native scenarios under study.

Fig. 4. Comparative environmental profiles per impact category between three bioenergy plants. Plant A - Codigestion (pig slurry + maize silage); Plant B - Monodigestion (pig slurry); Plant C - Monodigestion (maize silage).
were better compared with BS due to the reduction of ammonia emissions.

The results obtained regarding the environmental profile of the bioenergy plant under study (Plant A), which codigests maize silage and pig slurry, can be compared with those obtained in a previous study (Lijó et al., 2014), where two mono-digestion plants were assessed, one based on pig slurry as substrate (Plant B) and the other on maize silage (Plant C). It is important to remark that the cultivation of the maize used in plant A and in plant C is not exactly the same, since different amount of herbicides, water for irrigation, fertilisers (and thus, different derived emissions) are applied, resulting in different maize yield per hectare. Moreover, it is important to note that both plants A and C include a separation step of the raw digestate, resulting in liquid and solid fraction. In both plants, the solid fraction is stored, transported and applied on land as an organic fertiliser by means of manure spreader. The difference relies on the management of the liquid fraction because in plant A, differently to plant B, not all the digestate liquid fraction is recirculated to the digester, being part of this fraction also stored, delivered and used as a fertiliser. Liquid digestate is recirculated into the digester in order to keep dry matter concen- tration under 10% (Probiogás, 2011). Fig. 4 displays the compara- tive environmental effects between the three mentioned bioenergy plants. According to the results reported in Fig. 4, the use of maize for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant C) rendered the worst results in all impact categories. Nonetheless, the utilisation of pig slurry as a substrate for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant B) achieved the best environmental profile. The anaerobic co-digestion of both sub- strates (Plant A) was ranked in an intermediate position. The reason behind the performance of Plant B is mainly related with the absence of a cultivation step (SS1), which implied high

environmental burdens as well as environmental credits accrued by the avoided pig slurry conventional management and mineral fertilisation. As shown in Table 1, the specific biogas production of pig slurry is lower compared with maize. In addition, for the same amount of biogas, much more feedstock needs to be digested in plant B (Table 8).

In addition, in Table 8 it can also be seen that in Plant C less amount of feedstock is required in order to produce the same vol- ume of than Plant A. However, the higher electrical efficiency of Plant A implied lower specific biogas consumption. Moreover, environmental credits such as those from the avoided conventional management of pig slurry contributed to a better environmental profile in Plant A.

Additionally, the cumulative energy demand (CED) was calcu- lated in order to determine the quantity of non-renewable energy required in each biogas plant to produce the output electricity (Hischier et al., 2009). The results of CED are presented in Table 8 in two different ways. Firstly, global results including all processes defined within the system boundaries (see Fig. 1) are shown and then CED was calculated excluding the avoided pro- cesses in order to determine the real energy consumption of each plant. As expected, for the three plants under assessment the val- ues for CED were lower when avoided processes were included because of the environmental credits associated. Using these val- ues of CED (without the avoided processes), the energy efficiency of the bioenergy plants was calculated in terms of Energy Return on Investment (EROI), which is defined as the ratio of the energy delivered by a process to the energy used directly and indirectly in the same process. The results indicate that, although Plant B is the plant which less bioenergy produced per functional unit, the relationship between the energy input and output is higher in

comparison with the other two plants. The rationale behind these results is related to the non-renewable energy consumption during the cultivation step (SS1) in Plant A and C. Specifically, this subsys- tem provided around 90% of the burdens of CED when avoided pro- cesses were excluded from the calculations due to energy consumed in the agricultural activities (mainly due to the high energy input required for irrigation operations, mainly in electric- ity, diesel and machinery).

The comparison among BS and each alternative scenario under study (AS1, AS2 and AS3) is shown in Fig. 3.

As it can be seen, environmental credits derived from surplus heat valorisation (AS1) significantly influenced the definition of system profile, especially in energy related categories such as CC, OD and FD. The reduction of 80% in emissions when the storage tank of the liquid fraction of digestate is covered (AS2) had a posi- tive effect in impact categories such as CC due to avoided emis- sions of residual biogas as well as TA and ME due to the decrease of ammonia emissions. Fig. 3. Comparative environmental profiles per impact category between alter- native scenarios under study.

were better compared with BS due to the reduction of ammonia emissions.

The results obtained regarding the environmental profile of the bioenergy plant under study (Plant A), which codigests maize silage and pig slurry, can be compared with those obtained in a previous study (Lijó et al., 2014), where two mono-digestion plants were assessed, one based on pig slurry as substrate (Plant B) and the other on maize silage (Plant C). It is important to remark that the cultivation of the maize used in plant A and in plant C is not exactly the same, since different amount of herbicides, water for irrigation, fertilisers (and thus, different derived emissions) are applied, resulting in different maize yield per hectare. Moreover, it is important to note that both plants A and C include a separation step of the raw digestate, resulting in liquid and solid fraction. In both plants, the solid fraction is stored, transported and applied on land as an organic fertiliser by means of manure spreader. The difference relies on the management of the liquid fraction because in plant A, differently to plant B, not all the digestate liquid fraction is recirculated to the digester, being part of this fraction also stored, delivered and used as a fertiliser. Liquid digestate is recirculated into the digester in order to keep dry matter concen- tration under 10% (Probiogás, 2011). Fig. 4 displays the compara- tive environmental effects between the three mentioned bioenergy plants. According to the results reported in Fig. 4, the use of maize for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant C) rendered the worst results in all impact categories. Nonetheless, the utilisation of pig slurry as a substrate for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant B) achieved the best environmental profile. The anaerobic co-digestion of both sub- strates (Plant A) was ranked in an intermediate position. The reason behind the performance of Plant B is mainly related with the absence of a cultivation step (SS1), which implied high

environmental burdens as well as environmental credits accrued by the avoided pig slurry conventional management and mineral fertilisation. As shown in Table 1, the specific biogas production of pig slurry is lower compared with maize. In addition, for the same amount of biogas, much more feedstock needs to be digested in plant B (Table 8).

In addition, in Table 8 it can also be seen that in Plant C less amount of feedstock is required in order to produce the same vol- ume of than Plant A. However, the higher electrical efficiency of Plant A implied lower specific biogas consumption. Moreover, environmental credits such as those from the avoided conventional management of pig slurry contributed to a better environmental profile in Plant A.

Additionally, the cumulative energy demand (CED) was calcu- lated in order to determine the quantity of non-renewable energy required in each biogas plant to produce the output electricity (Hischier et al., 2009). The results of CED are presented in Table 8 in two different ways. Firstly, global results including all processes defined within the system boundaries (see Fig. 1) are shown and then CED was calculated excluding the avoided pro- cesses in order to determine the real energy consumption of each plant. As expected, for the three plants under assessment the val- ues for CED were lower when avoided processes were included because of the environmental credits associated. Using these val- ues of CED (without the avoided processes), the energy efficiency of the bioenergy plants was calculated in terms of Energy Return on Investment (EROI), which is defined as the ratio of the energy delivered by a process to the energy used directly and indirectly in the same process. The results indicate that, although Plant B is the plant which less bioenergy produced per functional unit, the relationship between the energy input and output is higher in

comparison with the other two plants. The rationale behind these results is related to the non-renewable energy consumption during the cultivation step (SS1) in Plant A and C. Specifically, this subsys- tem provided around 90% of the burdens of CED when avoided pro- cesses were excluded from the calculations due to energy consumed in the agricultural activities (mainly due to the high energy input required for irrigation operations, mainly in electric- ity, diesel and machinery).

related with the conventional management of pig slurry while the environmental burdens of electricity production are related with the high ratio of non-renewable sources in the Italian electric pro- file. In addition, the avoided mineral fertilisation due to the use of the digestate brings remarkable credits in ME (33%), due to avoided nitrate emissions to water.

The base scenario (BS) previously analysed was compared with different alternative scenarios in order to determine how potential improvement actions could affect the environmental profile of the biogas plant under study.

As mentioned before, heat is produced together with electricity in the CHP. As in many Italian biogas plans, electricity is totally sold to the Italian grid, while heat is only partially recycled in heat- ing the anaerobic reactors. The full exploitation of surplus heat is complicated since the amount of heat produced varies according to weather conditions and it can be only carry out when the recep- tor is close enough to the CHP. However, the surplus heat repre- sents a significant share of the total amount produced by the

CHP. This heat might be used to heat pigsties or nearby green- houses during the coldest months, to dry forage, to drive a small Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) turbine or an absorption chiller. In this alternative scenario (AS1), surplus heat was considered to be used in two greenhouses from October to March, resulting in environmental credits due to avoided heat produced in a diesel boiler. Each greenhouse has a global area of 1 ha and it is used to grown tomato. The infrastructure required for transmitting the heat to the greenhouses is considered negligible, since, according to our results, the contribution of the infrastructure corresponding to the biogas plant is up to 3% of the total impact produced.
3.2.2. Covering the liquid digestate storage tank (AS2)

Each fraction of the digestate (solid and liquid) is separately stored. Biogas as well as nitrogen-based emissions were identified since storage was considered in open tank for the liquid fraction and in pile for the solid fraction. In this alternative scenario (AS2), covering the storage tank of the digestate liquid fraction was proposed in order to decreased derived emissions (80%) as well as to take advantage of the residual biogas (ISPRA, 2008; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014). This biogas is recirculated to the pro- cess resulting in light increase (0.14%) of electricity production and avoided electricity from the grid. In addition, it was assumed that an airtight cover on the storage tank would decrease emissions of N-based compounds (Wulf et al., 2006). Therefore, only 20% of emissions computed for the storage of liquid fraction are realised in this alternative scenario.

Emissions during and after digestate application on arable land are highly influenced by the application practise used (Wulf et al., 2006). As base case, surface spreading was considered as applica- tion technique of digestate because it is the most widespread pro- cedure for organic fertilisers in the Po Valley (78%) (ISPRA, 2008). However, injection is also carried out but to a lesser extent. According to Wulf et al. (2006), when injection of the digestate is performed, ammonia emissions are nearly half of these from sur- face spreading with a splash plate, because of the decrease in ammonia volatilisation (Wulf et al., 2002a). However, nitrous oxide emissions are around 2.7 times higher due to the promotion of anaerobic sites (Wulf et al., 2002b). In addition, the consumption of diesel fuel is higher when injection is applied compared with surface spreading because the slurry tank is equipped with a cou- ple of anchors working at 20-25 cm depth. environmental burdens as well as environmental credits accrued by the avoided pig slurry conventional management and mineral fertilisation. As shown in Table 1, the specific biogas production of pig slurry is lower compared with maize. In addition, for the same amount of biogas, much more feedstock needs to be digested in plant B (Table 8).

In addition, in Table 8 it can also be seen that in Plant C less amount of feedstock is required in order to produce the same vol- ume of than Plant A. However, the higher electrical efficiency of Plant A implied lower specific biogas consumption. Moreover, environmental credits such as those from the avoided conventional management of pig slurry contributed to a better environmental profile in Plant A.

Additionally, the cumulative energy demand (CED) was calcu- lated in order to determine the quantity of non-renewable energy required in each biogas plant to produce the output electricity (Hischier et al., 2009). The results of CED are presented in Table 8 in two different ways. Firstly, global results including all processes defined within the system boundaries (see Fig. 1) are shown and then CED was calculated excluding the avoided pro- cesses in order to determine the real energy consumption of each plant. As expected, for the three plants under assessment the val- ues for CED were lower when avoided processes were included because of the environmental credits associated. Using these val- ues of CED (without the avoided processes), the energy efficiency of the bioenergy plants was calculated in terms of Energy Return on Investment (EROI), which is defined as the ratio of the energy delivered by a process to the energy used directly and indirectly in the same process. The results indicate that, although Plant B is the plant which less bioenergy produced per functional unit, the relationship between the energy input and output is higher in

comparison with the other two plants. The rationale behind these results is related to the non-renewable energy consumption during the cultivation step (SS1) in Plant A and C. Specifically, this subsys- tem provided around 90% of the burdens of CED when avoided pro- cesses were excluded from the calculations due to energy consumed in the agricultural activities (mainly due to the high energy input required for irrigation operations, mainly in electric- ity, diesel and machinery).

related with the conventional management of pig slurry while the environmental burdens of electricity production are related with the high ratio of non-renewable sources in the Italian electric pro- file. In addition, the avoided mineral fertilisation due to the use of the digestate brings remarkable credits in ME (33%), due to avoided nitrate emissions to water.

The base scenario (BS) previously analysed was compared with different alternative scenarios in order to determine how potential improvement actions could affect the environmental profile of the biogas plant under study.

As mentioned before, heat is produced together with electricity in the CHP. As in many Italian biogas plans, electricity is totally sold to the Italian grid, while heat is only partially recycled in heat- ing the anaerobic reactors. The full exploitation of surplus heat is complicated since the amount of heat produced varies according to weather conditions and it can be only carry out when the recep- tor is close enough to the CHP. However, the surplus heat repre- sents a significant share of the total amount produced by the

CHP. This heat might be used to heat pigsties or nearby green- houses during the coldest months, to dry forage, to drive a small Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) turbine or an absorption chiller. In this alternative scenario (AS1), surplus heat was considered to be used in two greenhouses from October to March, resulting in environmental credits due to avoided heat produced in a diesel boiler. Each greenhouse has a global area of 1 ha and it is used to grown tomato. The infrastructure required for transmitting the heat to the greenhouses is considered negligible, since, according to our results, the contribution of the infrastructure corresponding to the biogas plant is up to 3% of the total impact produced.
3.2.2. Covering the liquid digestate storage tank (AS2)

Each fraction of the digestate (solid and liquid) is separately stored. Biogas as well as nitrogen-based emissions were identified since storage was considered in open tank for the liquid fraction and in pile for the solid fraction. In this alternative scenario (AS2), covering the storage tank of the digestate liquid fraction was proposed in order to decreased derived emissions (80%) as well as to take advantage of the residual biogas (ISPRA, 2008; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014). This biogas is recirculated to the pro- cess resulting in light increase (0.14%) of electricity production and avoided electricity from the grid. In addition, it was assumed that an airtight cover on the storage tank would decrease emissions of N-based compounds (Wulf et al., 2006). Therefore, only 20% of emissions computed for the storage of liquid fraction are realised in this alternative scenario.

Emissions during and after digestate application on arable land are highly influenced by the application practise used (Wulf et al., 2006). As base case, surface spreading was considered as applica- tion technique of digestate because it is the most widespread pro- cedure for organic fertilisers in the Po Valley (78%) (ISPRA, 2008). However, injection is also carried out but to a lesser extent. According to Wulf et al. (2006), when injection of the digestate is performed, ammonia emissions are nearly half of these from sur- face spreading with a splash plate, because of the decrease in ammonia volatilisation (Wulf et al., 2002a). However, nitrous oxide emissions are around 2.7 times higher due to the promotion of anaerobic sites (Wulf et al., 2002b). In addition, the consumption of diesel fuel is higher when injection is applied compared with surface spreading because the slurry tank is equipped with a cou- ple of anchors working at 20-25 cm depth. The comparison among BS and each alternative scenario under study (AS1, AS2 and AS3) is shown in Fig. 3.

As it can be seen, environmental credits derived from surplus heat valorisation (AS1) significantly influenced the definition of system profile, especially in energy related categories such as CC, OD and FD. The reduction of 80% in emissions when the storage tank of the liquid fraction of digestate is covered (AS2) had a posi- tive effect in impact categories such as CC due to avoided emis- sions of residual biogas as well as TA and ME due to the decrease of ammonia emissions. Fig. 3. Comparative environmental profiles per impact category between alter- native scenarios under study.

were better compared with BS due to the reduction of ammonia emissions.

The results obtained regarding the environmental profile of the bioenergy plant under study (Plant A), which codigests maize silage and pig slurry, can be compared with those obtained in a previous study (Lijó et al., 2014), where two mono-digestion plants were assessed, one based on pig slurry as substrate (Plant B) and the other on maize silage (Plant C). It is important to remark that the cultivation of the maize used in plant A and in plant C is not exactly the same, since different amount of herbicides, water for irrigation, fertilisers (and thus, different derived emissions) are applied, resulting in different maize yield per hectare. Moreover, it is important to note that both plants A and C include a separation step of the raw digestate, resulting in liquid and solid fraction. In both plants, the solid fraction is stored, transported and applied on land as an organic fertiliser by means of manure spreader. The difference relies on the management of the liquid fraction because in plant A, differently to plant B, not all the digestate liquid fraction is recirculated to the digester, being part of this fraction also stored, delivered and used as a fertiliser. Liquid digestate is recirculated into the digester in order to keep dry matter concen- tration under 10% (Probiogás, 2011). Fig. 4 displays the compara- tive environmental effects between the three mentioned bioenergy plants. According to the results reported in Fig. 4, the use of maize for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant C) rendered the worst results in all impact categories. Nonetheless, the utilisation of pig slurry as a substrate for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant B) achieved the best environmental profile. The anaerobic co-digestion of both sub- strates (Plant A) was ranked in an intermediate position. related with the conventional management of pig slurry while the environmental burdens of electricity production are related with the high ratio of non-renewable sources in the Italian electric pro- file. In addition, the avoided mineral fertilisation due to the use of the digestate brings remarkable credits in ME (33%), due to avoided nitrate emissions to water.

The base scenario (BS) previously analysed was compared with different alternative scenarios in order to determine how potential improvement actions could affect the environmental profile of the biogas plant under study.

As mentioned before, heat is produced together with electricity in the CHP. As in many Italian biogas plans, electricity is totally sold to the Italian grid, while heat is only partially recycled in heat- ing the anaerobic reactors. The full exploitation of surplus heat is complicated since the amount of heat produced varies according to weather conditions and it can be only carry out when the recep- tor is close enough to the CHP. However, the surplus heat repre- sents a significant share of the total amount produced by the

CHP. This heat might be used to heat pigsties or nearby green- houses during the coldest months, to dry forage, to drive a small Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) turbine or an absorption chiller. In this alternative scenario (AS1), surplus heat was considered to be used in two greenhouses from October to March, resulting in environmental credits due to avoided heat produced in a diesel boiler. Each greenhouse has a global area of 1 ha and it is used to grown tomato. The infrastructure required for transmitting the heat to the greenhouses is considered negligible, since, according to our results, the contribution of the infrastructure corresponding to the biogas plant is up to 3% of the total impact produced.
3.2.2. Covering the liquid digestate storage tank (AS2)

Each fraction of the digestate (solid and liquid) is separately stored. Biogas as well as nitrogen-based emissions were identified since storage was considered in open tank for the liquid fraction and in pile for the solid fraction. In this alternative scenario (AS2), covering the storage tank of the digestate liquid fraction was proposed in order to decreased derived emissions (80%) as well as to take advantage of the residual biogas (ISPRA, 2008; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014). This biogas is recirculated to the pro- cess resulting in light increase (0.14%) of electricity production and avoided electricity from the grid. In addition, it was assumed that an airtight cover on the storage tank would decrease emissions of N-based compounds (Wulf et al., 2006). Therefore, only 20% of emissions computed for the storage of liquid fraction are realised in this alternative scenario.

Emissions during and after digestate application on arable land are highly influenced by the application practise used (Wulf et al., 2006). As base case, surface spreading was considered as applica- tion technique of digestate because it is the most widespread pro- cedure for organic fertilisers in the Po Valley (78%) (ISPRA, 2008). However, injection is also carried out but to a lesser extent. According to Wulf et al. (2006), when injection of the digestate is performed, ammonia emissions are nearly half of these from sur- face spreading with a splash plate, because of the decrease in ammonia volatilisation (Wulf et al., 2002a). However, nitrous oxide emissions are around 2.7 times higher due to the promotion of anaerobic sites (Wulf et al., 2002b). In addition, the consumption of diesel fuel is higher when injection is applied compared with surface spreading because the slurry tank is equipped with a cou- ple of anchors working at 20-25 cm depth. The comparison among BS and each alternative scenario under study (AS1, AS2 and AS3) is shown in Fig. 3.

As it can be seen, environmental credits derived from surplus heat valorisation (AS1) significantly influenced the definition of system profile, especially in energy related categories such as CC, OD and FD. The reduction of 80% in emissions when the storage tank of the liquid fraction of digestate is covered (AS2) had a posi- tive effect in impact categories such as CC due to avoided emis- sions of residual biogas as well as TA and ME due to the decrease of ammonia emissions. Finally, the increase of nitrous oxide emis- sions related with liquid fraction of digestate injection in arable land (AS3) reduced the positive effect achieved in CC due to the increased emissions of nitrous oxide. However, the results for TA
Fig. 3. Comparative environmental profiles per impact category between alter- native scenarios under study.

Fig. 4. Comparative environmental profiles per impact category between three bioenergy plants. Plant A - Codigestion (pig slurry + maize silage); Plant B - Monodigestion (pig slurry); Plant C - Monodigestion (maize silage).
were better compared with BS due to the reduction of ammonia emissions.

The results obtained regarding the environmental profile of the bioenergy plant under study (Plant A), which codigests maize silage and pig slurry, can be compared with those obtained in a previous study (Lijó et al., 2014), where two mono-digestion plants were assessed, one based on pig slurry as substrate (Plant B) and the other on maize silage (Plant C). It is important to remark that the cultivation of the maize used in plant A and in plant C is not exactly the same, since different amount of herbicides, water for irrigation, fertilisers (and thus, different derived emissions) are applied, resulting in different maize yield per hectare. Moreover, it is important to note that both plants A and C include a separation step of the raw digestate, resulting in liquid and solid fraction. In both plants, the solid fraction is stored, transported and applied on land as an organic fertiliser by means of manure spreader. The difference relies on the management of the liquid fraction because in plant A, differently to plant B, not all the digestate liquid fraction is recirculated to the digester, being part of this fraction also stored, delivered and used as a fertiliser. Liquid digestate is recirculated into the digester in order to keep dry matter concen- tration under 10% (Probiogás, 2011). Fig. 4 displays the compara- tive environmental effects between the three mentioned bioenergy plants. According to the results reported in Fig. 4, the use of maize for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant C) rendered the worst results in all impact categories. Nonetheless, the utilisation of pig slurry as a substrate for anaerobic mono-digestion (Plant B) achieved the best environmental profile. The anaerobic co-digestion of both sub- strates (Plant A) was ranked in an intermediate position. The reason behind the performance of Plant B is mainly related with the absence of a cultivation step (SS1), which implied high

environmental burdens as well as environmental credits accrued by the avoided pig slurry conventional management and mineral fertilisation. As shown in Table 1, the specific biogas production of pig slurry is lower compared with maize. In addition, for the same amount of biogas, much more feedstock needs to be digested in plant B (Table 8).

In addition, in Table 8 it can also be seen that in Plant C less amount of feedstock is required in order to produce the same vol- ume of than Plant A. However, the higher electrical efficiency of Plant A implied lower specific biogas consumption. Moreover, environmental credits such as those from the avoided conventional management of pig slurry contributed to a better environmental profile in Plant A.

Additionally, the cumulative energy demand (CED) was calcu- lated in order to determine the quantity of non-renewable energy required in each biogas plant to produce the output electricity (Hischier et al., 2009). The results of CED are presented in Table 8 in two different ways. Firstly, global results including all processes defined within the system boundaries (see Fig. 1) are shown and then CED was calculated excluding the avoided pro- cesses in order to determine the real energy consumption of each plant. As expected, for the three plants under assessment the val- ues for CED were lower when avoided processes were included because of the environmental credits associated. Using these val- ues of CED (without the avoided processes), the energy efficiency of the bioenergy plants was calculated in terms of Energy Return on Investment (EROI), which is defined as the ratio of the energy delivered by a process to the energy used directly and indirectly in the same process. The results indicate that, although Plant B is the plant which less bioenergy produced per functional unit, the relationship between the energy input and output is higher in

comparison with the other two plants. The rationale behind these results is related to the non-renewable energy consumption during the cultivation step (SS1) in Plant A and C. Specifically, this subsys- tem provided around 90% of the burdens of CED when avoided pro- cesses were excluded from the calculations due to energy consumed in the agricultural activities (mainly due to the high energy input required for irrigation operations, mainly in electric- ity, diesel and machinery).

The comparison among BS and each alternative scenario under study (AS1, AS2 and AS3) is shown in Fig. 3.

As it can be seen, environmental credits derived from surplus heat valorisation (AS1) significantly influenced the definition of system profile, especially in energy related categories such as CC, OD and FD. The reduction of 80% in emissions when the storage tank of the liquid fraction of digestate is covered (AS2) had a posi- tive effect in impact categories such as CC due to avoided emis- sions of residual biogas as well as TA and ME due to the decrease of ammonia emissions. Finally, the increase of nitrous oxide emis- sions related with liquid fraction of digestate injection in arable land (AS3) reduced the positive effect achieved in CC due to the increased emissions of nitrous oxide. Fig. 3. Comparative environmental profiles per impact category between alter- native scenarios under study.
